lessons.title: Greetings
lessons.culturalNote: isiXhosa is famous for its three click sounds: c (dental), x (lateral), and q (palatal). Greet one person with "Molo" and a group with "Molweni".

exercises.prompt: How do you greet a group of people?

lessons.title: Yes, No & Politeness
lessons.culturalNote: "Enkosi" (thank you) is one of the most important words you'll learn. "Uxolo" means both "sorry" and "excuse me".

exercises.meaning: Excuse me, may I have water.

lessons.title: Family & Respect
lessons.culturalNote: As in Zulu, you show respect by addressing elders as "Tata" (father) or "Mama" (mother). Mandela was affectionately called "Tata" by the nation.

exercises.prompt: How do you respectfully address an older man?

exercises.meaning: This child is five years old.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–10
lessons.culturalNote: Like Zulu, Xhosa numbers change shape with the noun they count. These are common counting forms used by learners.

lessons.title: Food & Drink
lessons.culturalNote: Offering food and drink is a sign of welcome. "Ndicela amanzi" (please may I have water) is a polite, useful phrase.

exercises.meaning: Please may I have tea and bread.

lessons.title: Directions
lessons.culturalNote: Begin with "Uxolo" (excuse me) when asking for directions. "Ekunene" is right and "ekhohlo" is left.

lessons.title: Introducing Yourself
lessons.culturalNote: Xhosa greetings build trust quickly. 'Igama lam ngu...' means 'My name is...'. 'Ndiyavuya ukukwazi' is 'Nice to meet you'.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “What is your name?”?

lessons.title: At the Shop
lessons.culturalNote: At the market or spaza, 'Yimalini?' (How much?) and 'Ndicela...' (Please / I request) go a long way.

lessons.title: Numbers 1–5
lessons.culturalNote: Xhosa numbers, like Zulu, change with what you count. These are common forms heard when counting items.

lessons.title: Family & Respect
lessons.culturalNote: Respect for elders is central. Address an older woman as 'mama' and an older man as 'tata', even if they are not your parents.

lessons.title: Days of the Week
lessons.culturalNote: The Xhosa week starts with uMvulo (Monday) and ends with iCawa (Sunday, which also means 'church'). Several names build on counting, like Zulu.

lessons.title: Colours
lessons.culturalNote: Colour words agree with the noun in Xhosa. 'Luhlaza' covers green and blue; add 'okwengca' (like grass) for green or 'okwesibhakabhaka' (like sky) for blue.

lessons.title: Feelings
lessons.culturalNote: Say how you feel with 'ndi-' (I). 'Ndilambile' (I'm hungry) and 'ndinxaniwe' (I'm thirsty) are everyday phrases — the 'nx' in nxaniwe is a click.

exercises.prompt: How do you say “I am tired”?

lessons.title: The Body
lessons.culturalNote: Body words show up in greetings about health. Note the classes: 'intloko' (head) and 'isandla' (hand) take different plural forms.

lessons.title: Time & Routine
lessons.culturalNote: Mark the day with 'kusasa' (morning) and 'ebusuku' (night). 'Namhlanje' (today), 'izolo' (yesterday) and 'ngomso' (tomorrow) anchor any plan.

lessons.title: Animals
lessons.culturalNote: Animals feature in Xhosa praise poetry (izibongo). 'Inkomo' (cattle) carries cultural weight in ceremonies, much as in Zulu.

lessons.title: Around Town
lessons.culturalNote: Find your way by asking 'Iphi...?' (Where is...?). 'Isikolo' (school) and 'isibhedlele' (hospital) are good landmarks to know.

lessons.title: Numbers 6–10
lessons.culturalNote: Higher numbers in Xhosa get longer: 'isithandathu' (six), 'isixhenxe' (seven). The 'x' in isixhenxe is a lateral click.

reading.title: Molo, Lindiwe!
reading.intro: Two friends greet each other. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Hello Lindiwe. My name is Ayanda.
I am well, thank you. And you?
I am also well. Go well!

reading.title: Molo, Thandi!
reading.intro: Two friends greet each other. Tap any line to hear it.

lines.en: Hello Thandi. My name is Sabelo.
I am well, thank you. And you?
I am also well. Stay well!
